title: Household Systems, Troubleshooting & the Invisible Infrastructure

required.title: [Tool] Non-contact voltage tester (voltage tick or voltage pen)
[Tools] Multimeter (basic digital), screwdrivers (insulated handles), wire strippers, pliers, flashlight, adjustable wrench, pipe wrench, plunger, drain auger (snake)

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: "This Old House" — electrical and plumbing repair videos

supplementary.title: [Book] Structures: Or Why Things Don't Fall Down
supplementary.author: J.E. Gordon

competence_markers.description: The student can describe how electricity reaches their home from generation through the breaker panel to individual circuits
The student understands the specific mechanisms of electrical injury and can explain why water increases the danger
The student can safely replace a light switch, light fixture, and outlet with power confirmed off (voltage tester verified)
The student can locate and operate the main water shutoff and individual fixture shutoffs in their home
The student understands supply (pressure), drainage (gravity), and venting systems in plumbing
The student can replace an HVAC filter and explain why filter maintenance matters
The student can approach a system failure using systematic troubleshooting: check the obvious, form a hypothesis, test, and narrow possibilities
The student has mapped the electrical circuits in their home

content.why_this_matters:
Your home is a machine. Behind the walls, under the floors, and above the ceilings, a network of systems carries electricity, water, drainage, and heated or cooled air to every room. Most people live in these machines every day without understanding how they work. When something fails — a tripped breaker, a clogged drain, a furnace that will not start — they call someone and wait, because they have no idea what went wrong or what to do about it.

You are going to become the person who understands. Not at the level of a licensed electrician or plumber — those are professional specialties that require years of training. But at the level of a competent homeowner who can identify components, perform basic maintenance, diagnose common problems, communicate intelligently with professionals, and — critically — know the boundary between what you can safely do yourself and what requires an expert.

This knowledge is directly connected to your independence. The person who can reset a tripped breaker, replace a light switch, unclog a drain, change a furnace filter, and identify the source of a water leak saves thousands of dollars over a lifetime and avoids the helplessness of waiting for someone else to solve problems in their own home. The person who cannot is dependent on others for the basic functioning of the place where they live.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Household electrical understanding** — service entrance, main panel, branch circuits, breakers, outlets, switches, and grounding
2. **Electrical safety** — the specific mechanisms by which electricity injures and kills, and the protections designed to prevent it
3. **Basic electrical work** — replacing a light switch, a light fixture, and an outlet under supervision (power confirmed off)
4. **Plumbing fundamentals** — supply systems, drainage systems, venting, shutoff valves, and common failure points
5. **HVAC awareness** — how heating and cooling systems work, filter maintenance, and basic system monitoring
6. **Motors and generators** — the relationship between electricity and mechanical motion
7. **Systematic troubleshooting** — a methodical approach to diagnosing why something stopped working
content.core_content:
### Part 1: How Electricity Reaches Your Home

Electricity travels a long path to reach the outlet where you plug in a lamp.

**Generation:** A power plant converts energy (burning fuel, nuclear fission, falling water, wind, sunlight) into electricity. The generator produces electricity at high voltage.

**Transmission:** High-voltage power lines carry electricity from the power plant to your area. High voltage is used for transmission because it reduces energy loss over long distances.

**Distribution:** A transformer near your home steps the voltage down to household level: 240 volts for the main service and 120 volts for most circuits.

**Service entrance:** The electricity enters your home through the service entrance — two hot wires (each carrying 120 volts) and a neutral wire. Together, the two hot wires provide 240 volts for heavy appliances (electric stove, dryer, water heater, central air conditioning).

**Main panel (breaker box):** The service entrance feeds the main panel. Inside the panel, the electricity is divided into branch circuits — individual circuits that each serve a section of the house. Each branch circuit has its own breaker — a safety device that trips (disconnects) if the circuit carries more current than it is rated for.

**Branch circuits:** Each circuit runs from the panel to a group of outlets, switches, and fixtures. A typical home has 15-amp circuits (for bedrooms, living rooms) and 20-amp circuits (for kitchen, bathroom, garage — areas with higher-power devices). Some large appliances have dedicated circuits — one breaker serving one appliance.

**The path:** Power plant to transmission lines to transformer to service entrance to main panel to branch circuit to outlet to your lamp — and back through the neutral wire to the panel and out to the transformer. A complete circuit, just like the battery circuit you built in Stretching Branches, but enormously more powerful.

### Part 2: Electrical Safety — How Electricity Hurts and Kills

This is the most important section in this packet. Read it. Understand it. Remember it.

**The mechanism of injury:** Electricity injures and kills by flowing through the body. Specifically, it is current (measured in amperes) flowing through the heart that is lethal. As little as 0.1 ampere (100 milliamps) through the heart can cause ventricular fibrillation — the heart quivers instead of pumping, and the person dies without immediate defibrillation.

**Voltage drives current.** Voltage is electrical pressure. By itself, voltage does not kill — but it drives the current that does. The 120 volts in a household outlet can drive enough current through the human body to be lethal, depending on the resistance of the path.

**Resistance is your protection — and water destroys it.** Your skin, when dry, has high electrical resistance — roughly 100,000 ohms. This limits the current that 120 volts can push through your body to a small and usually non-lethal amount. But wet skin has a resistance as low as 1,000 ohms — one hundred times lower. At that resistance, 120 volts can drive 120 milliamps through your body — more than enough to kill. This is why water and electricity together are extremely dangerous, and why bathrooms and kitchens have special safety devices.

**The ground wire:** Every modern outlet has three connections: hot (carries current to the device), neutral (returns current to the panel), and ground (a safety wire that provides a low-resistance path to ground in case of a fault). If a hot wire inside an appliance comes loose and touches the metal case, the ground wire carries the fault current to the panel and trips the breaker — before someone touches the metal case and becomes the path to ground instead.

**GFCI outlets (Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters):** Required by code in bathrooms, kitchens, garages, outdoors, and anywhere near water. A GFCI continuously compares the current flowing out on the hot wire with the current returning on the neutral wire. If there is any difference — even a tiny one, as small as 5 milliamps — it means current is flowing somewhere it should not be (possibly through a person). The GFCI trips in approximately 1/40 of a second — fast enough to prevent lethal shock in most cases. Test your GFCI outlets monthly by pressing the test button.

**The absolute rule for working on household electrical systems:** Before touching any wire, terminal, or connection, confirm that the circuit is de-energized. Turn off the breaker. Then test with a non-contact voltage tester to confirm there is no voltage. Then test again. People have died because they turned off the wrong breaker, or because someone else turned the breaker back on while they were working. Lock the breaker panel if possible, and always test before you touch.

### Part 3: Basic Electrical Work

Under your mentor's supervision, with the circuit confirmed de-energized at the breaker and verified with a voltage tester, you will perform three basic electrical tasks.

**Replacing a light switch:** Turn off the breaker. Test with voltage tester. Remove the cover plate. Remove the two screws holding the switch in the box. Pull the switch out and note which wires connect to which terminals (take a photograph before disconnecting). Disconnect the wires. Connect them to the new switch in the same configuration. Push the switch back into the box. Replace the screws and cover plate. Turn on the breaker. Test.

**Replacing a light fixture:** Turn off the breaker. Test with voltage tester. Remove the old fixture. Note the wire connections: black (hot) to black, white (neutral) to white, green or bare (ground) to ground. Disconnect. Connect the new fixture with wire nuts: black to black, white to white, ground to ground. Mount the new fixture. Turn on the breaker. Test.

**Replacing an outlet:** Turn off the breaker. Test with voltage tester. Remove the cover plate and mounting screws. Pull the outlet out. Note wire connections (photograph before disconnecting). Disconnect. Connect to the new outlet: hot (black) to brass-colored screw, neutral (white) to silver-colored screw, ground (green or bare) to green screw. Push back into box. Replace screws and cover plate. Turn on breaker. Test.

**The principle behind all three:** You are not designing new circuits. You are replacing one component with an identical component, connecting the same wires to the same terminals. This is within the competence of any careful person. But the safety protocol — breaker off, voltage tester, test before touch — is absolutely non-negotiable.

### Part 4: Plumbing Systems

**Supply system:** Clean water enters your home under pressure (typically 40-80 PSI) through a main supply line. A main shutoff valve allows you to stop all water flow to the house. The supply line splits to serve cold water directly and hot water through the water heater. Supply pipes are typically copper, PEX (cross-linked polyethylene), or in older homes, galvanized steel.

Individual fixtures (sinks, toilets, washing machines) have their own shutoff valves — small valves near the fixture that allow you to stop water to that fixture without shutting off the whole house. Know where every shutoff valve is in your home.

**Drainage system:** Dirty water leaves through drain pipes by gravity. Drain pipes slope downward (typically 1/4 inch per foot) so water flows to the sewer or septic system. Every drain has a trap — a U-shaped section of pipe that holds a small amount of water. This water seal prevents sewer gases from entering the house through the drain. The trap is why you see a curved pipe under every sink.

**Vent system:** Drains also need air to flow properly. Try pouring water out of an upside-down bottle — it glugs and flows slowly because air cannot get in as water comes out. Turn the bottle right-side-up and it pours smoothly because air enters above the water. Drain pipes have vent pipes that extend up through the roof, allowing air into the drainage system so water flows freely. If drains gurgle or drain slowly, a blocked vent may be the cause.

**Common plumbing problems:**
- *Clogged drain:* Hair, soap, food scraps accumulate in the trap or drain pipe. First try a plunger (creates pressure waves that dislodge clogs). If that fails, use a drain auger (snake) — a flexible cable that reaches into the pipe and physically breaks up or retrieves the clog. Chemical drain cleaners are a last resort — they are corrosive, dangerous, and can damage pipes.
- *Running toilet:* The toilet tank uses a fill valve (refills the tank after flushing) and a flapper valve (seals the bottom of the tank). A running toilet usually means the flapper is not sealing properly — it is worn, warped, or has debris under it. Replacing a flapper is a 5-minute, $5 repair.
- *Dripping faucet:* Usually a worn washer, O-ring, or cartridge inside the faucet body. Shut off the water at the fixture shutoff valve, disassemble the faucet, identify the worn part, replace it, reassemble. The specific process depends on the faucet type (compression, ball, cartridge, or disc).

### Part 5: HVAC — Heating, Ventilation & Air Conditioning

**Forced-air systems (most common):** A furnace or heat pump heats or cools air. A blower pushes that air through a network of ducts to every room. The air exits through supply registers (the vents in your rooms). It returns to the system through return air grilles (the larger grilles, usually in hallways or central locations). The thermostat controls the cycle — when the temperature drops below (in heating) or rises above (in cooling) the set point, the system runs until the temperature is satisfied.

**Filter maintenance:** The air filter catches dust, pollen, and debris before it enters the equipment. A clogged filter restricts airflow, which reduces efficiency, increases energy costs, and can damage the equipment (the system works harder to move air through a clogged filter). Replace or clean the filter every 1-3 months. This is the single most impactful HVAC maintenance task and one of the simplest.

**Basic HVAC troubleshooting:**
- System does not start: Is the thermostat set correctly? Is the breaker on? Is the power switch on the unit turned on? (Many furnaces have a light switch nearby that controls power to the unit.)
- System runs but does not heat/cool effectively: Is the filter clean? Are all supply registers open? Are return air grilles unblocked?
- Strange noises: A squealing blower may need a belt or bearing. Banging ductwork may be thermal expansion. Clicking at the furnace during startup is normal (igniter). Repeated clicking without ignition indicates an ignition problem.

### Part 6: Motors and Generators

A motor converts electricity into rotation. A generator converts rotation into electricity. They are the same device running in opposite directions.

**Inside a motor:** A coil of wire sits inside a magnetic field. When electricity flows through the coil, the magnetic field pushes on the current-carrying wire, causing the coil to rotate. This rotation is the motor's output — it drives a fan, a pump, a drill, a washing machine drum, a car wheel (in electric vehicles).

**Inside a generator:** A coil of wire is rotated inside a magnetic field by some external force (a turbine, an engine, a hand crank). The rotating coil in the magnetic field generates a flow of electricity. This is how virtually all electricity in the world is produced — a generator turned by steam (from burning fuel or nuclear fission), water (hydroelectric), or wind.

This duality — motor and generator as the same device in reverse — is one of the most elegant ideas in physics. It means that understanding one gives you understanding of the other. The motor in your shop drill and the generator at the power plant operate on the same principle, at different scales.

### Part 7: Systematic Troubleshooting

When something stops working, resist the urge to guess. Instead, follow a method:

1. **What changed?** The system was working before. Something changed. What? Did you do something different? Did weather change? Did someone else work on it? The most recent change is the most likely cause.
2. **Check the obvious first.** Is it plugged in? Is the switch on? Is the breaker tripped? Is the shutoff valve open? More than half of all "failures" are solved by checking the obvious.
3. **Form a hypothesis.** Based on the symptoms, what is the most likely cause? The furnace clicks but does not ignite — the most likely cause is a failed igniter or a dirty flame sensor, not a cracked heat exchanger.
4. **Test the simplest hypothesis first.** If you suspect a tripped breaker, check the breaker panel — it takes 30 seconds. If you suspect a failed motor, that is a harder test. Start simple.
5. **Eliminate possibilities.** Each test either confirms or eliminates a possibility. If the breaker is fine, the problem is downstream. If the filter is clean, the airflow restriction is elsewhere. Narrow the field systematically.
6. **Know when to call a professional.** If your troubleshooting leads to a complex failure (a failed control board, a refrigerant leak, a main sewer line clog), you have done valuable work — you know what the problem is and can communicate it clearly to the professional. You save their diagnostic time and your money.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Electrical Safety Protocol
**Developing well:** The student follows the full safety protocol (breaker off, voltage tester confirmation, test the tester, test again) every single time without reminders. They can explain why each step is necessary. They understand the lethal mechanism of electrical injury and can explain why wet conditions increase the danger.

**Developing slowly:** The student knows the protocol but skips steps when they "know" the power is off. They cannot explain the injury mechanism beyond "electricity is dangerous."

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you been following the full protocol yourself every single time? One shortcut from you teaches the student that shortcuts are acceptable. Have you explained the physics (current through the heart, resistance reduction from moisture) in terms the student can understand?

### 2. Basic Electrical Work
**Developing well:** The student can replace a switch, fixture, and outlet safely and correctly. Wire connections are secure. The component works when power is restored. The student can explain what each wire does (hot, neutral, ground) and why the connections go where they do.

**Developing slowly:** The student can physically perform the task but does not understand the wire functions. Connections are insecure. The student confuses hot and neutral.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you explained the color coding clearly (black=hot to brass screw, white=neutral to silver screw, green/bare=ground to green screw)? Has the student handled enough wire connections to develop the fine motor skill required for secure connections?

### 3. Plumbing Knowledge
**Developing well:** The student can locate every shutoff valve, explain the supply/drainage/vent system, and perform at least one basic repair (flapper, drain clearing, washer replacement). They understand the difference between pressure-driven supply and gravity-driven drainage.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot locate shutoff valves without help. They confuse supply and drainage systems. They have not performed a successful repair.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you toured the plumbing system together, tracing pipes from main shutoff to fixtures? Have you shown the student the trap under a sink and explained its purpose? Has the student performed a real repair, not just watched you do one?

### 4. Systematic Troubleshooting
**Developing well:** When presented with a problem, the student pauses, thinks, checks the obvious, forms a hypothesis, and tests it before taking action. They narrate their reasoning. They change one variable at a time.

**Developing slowly:** The student guesses, tries random fixes, or panics. They cannot articulate a systematic approach.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled systematic troubleshooting enough times for the student to internalize the pattern? Have you narrated your reasoning out loud during real troubleshooting events? Create practice troubleshooting challenges (deliberately trip a breaker, close a shutoff valve, disconnect a wire in a battery circuit) and let the student practice the method.

### 5. HVAC Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can locate the air filter, determine the correct size, replace it, and explain why clean filters matter. They can describe the basic forced-air system cycle (thermostat signals, equipment heats/cools air, blower pushes through ducts, returns to equipment).

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot locate the filter or does not understand the basic system cycle.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you shown the student the complete system — furnace/heat pump, ductwork, registers, return air, thermostat? Have you replaced a filter together?
